events: A king ordered his three sons, Pedro, Diego, and Juan, to seek their fortunes.
The three brothers took different directions but agreed to meet at a certain place in the forest.
Don Juan met an old man on the road who gave him bread and told him to divide it among the monkeys guarding the palace gate.
Don Juan entered the palace and was greeted by a big monkey who said he would marry Don Juan's daughter Chonguita.
The archbishop of the monkeys married Don Juan and Chonguita without delay.
Don Juan asked permission to leave with his wife Chonguita, who was forced to accompany him.
When Don Juan met his brothers at the agreed place, he couldn't say a word about his monkey wife.
Don Pedro and Don Diego were surprised by Chonguita's appearance and teased their brother Don Juan.
The king fainted upon learning that one of his sons had married a monkey but later accepted it as God's will.
The king ordered each couple to make him an embroidered coat within three days, hoping Chonguita would fail.
Chonguita surprised everyone by making the prettiest embroidered coat of all.
The king then ordered his daughters-in-law to make him a cap within two days under penalty of death.
Chonguita again made the most beautiful cap, and her husband Don Juan was crowned king after drawing the prettiest picture on the palace walls.
Don Juan became angry with Chonguita during a feast and hurled her against the wall, but she was transformed into a beautiful woman.
The king called his three sons to him and told them to seek their fortunes.
Don Juan came across an old man on the road who gave him a loaf of bread with instructions to divide it among the monkeys guarding the gate to the palace.
Don Juan entered the palace and saw a big monkey who told him he would marry Chonguita, the monkey's daughter.
Don Juan and Chonguita were married without delay by the archbishop of the monkeys.
Chonguita's mother suggested that Don Juan take his wife with him when he left to meet his brothers at their agreed-upon place.
When Don Juan met his brothers and their wives, he was overcome with shame because Chonguita was a monkey.
The king fainted from shock when he learned that one of his sons had married a monkey.
The king ordered each couple to make him an embroidered coat within three days, with the penalty of death for failure.
Chonguita's coat was the prettiest of all, disappointing the king's hope that she would fail.
The king ordered his daughters-in-law to embroider a cap for him within two days under penalty of death if they failed.
Chonguita again proved herself skilled and talented by creating the most beautiful cap of all.
The king ordered his daughters-in-law to draw a picture on the walls of his chamber within three days, with the husband of the winner becoming his successor on the throne.
Chonguita's picture was declared a masterpiece by the king, and Don Juan was chosen as his successor.
Don Juan became angry during a feast in honor of his coronation and hurled Chonguita against the wall, but she was transformed into a beautiful woman.
King Alfonso ruled with wisdom and justice in the grand kingdom of Azura.
He had three sons, Pedro, Diego, and Juan, who were as dear to him as the stars in the night sky.
The king summoned his sons and bade them set out from the kingdom to seek their fortunes.
They must agree on a meeting place in the forest before setting out.
After many days of walking, Don Juan chanced upon an old man on the road, who handed him bread and instructed him to divide it among the monkeys guarding the palace gate.
Don Juan did as he was told and entered the palace, where he met Chonguita, the beautiful daughter of the monkey king.
The archbishop of the monkeys performed a grand ceremony, uniting Don Juan and Chonguita in matrimony.
Don Juan requested permission from his wife to visit their brothers at the agreed meeting place.
Chonguita's mother, the queen of the monkeys, insisted that her daughter accompany Don Juan on this journey.
The king fainted from shock upon learning of his youngest son's union with a monkey.
A youth herded buffaloes and noticed a she-dog visiting a ravine every day at noon.
The youth hid himself to watch the dog and saw it transform into a beautiful maiden who bathed in the water.
When the maiden finished bathing, she put on her dog skin and returned to the village as a dog.
The youth followed the dog and discovered which house it entered.
The youth's parents decided it was time for him to marry, but he announced that he wanted to marry the she-dog instead of a human girl.
Everyone laughed at the youth's idea, but his parents eventually agreed to let him have his way.
The youth asked for the she-dog as his bride and gave the name of the man who owned her.
The master of the dog laughed at the idea but accepted a bride's price for her.
A day was fixed for the wedding, and the ceremony took place with the she-dog as the bride.
Every night, the bride would transform back into a maiden and leave the house while her husband slept.
The husband discovered his wife's secret and caught her one night, burning the dog skin in the fire.
The wife remained human but was of extraordinary beauty, which became known throughout the village.
A friend of the youth, Jitu, decided to marry a dog himself after seeing his friend's success.
Jitu's marriage ended in disaster when his bride broke loose and ran away, forcing him to give up and eventually leading to his death by hanging.
A young man tended to his father's buffaloes in their village.
He noticed a peculiar sight: every day at noon, a she-dog would visit a nearby ravine.
The dog shed its skin, revealing a beautiful maiden within.
The young man followed the maiden back to her house in the village.
He discovered which house the maiden entered and learned its owner's name.
When his parents announced that it was time for him to marry, he surprised them by declaring that he wanted a dog as his wife.
The villagers laughed at his notion, but the young man remained resolute.
A wedding was arranged, and the ceremony took place with great fanfare.
The young man's party escorted the 'bride' back to their home, where she would transform into a maiden every night.
However, her nocturnal escapades soon caught the attention of her husband, who lay in wait one evening and threw the dog skin into the fire.
The news spread throughout the village, and people marveled at the young man's wisdom in choosing a dog as his wife.
Jitu, however, was not so fortunate; he convinced his family to arrange a union with one of their own dogs.
The 'bride' party set off towards Jitu's home, but she broke free and chased after them.
Jitu was too ashamed to speak for days and eventually took his own life by hanging himself from a tree branch.
A young herdsman tended to his flock of buffaloes and often wondered about a she-dog that visited a nearby ravine every day.
The herdsman hid behind a bush and watched as the dog transformed into a beautiful maiden at noon.
The maiden bathed in the pools of water, revealing her beauty to the herdsman.
The herdsman followed the maiden into the village and learned that she lived with a kind old man.
The herdsman's parents began to speak of marriage, but he refused to marry a human girl and instead wanted to wed the maiden who transformed into a dog.
The herdsman's family and friends laughed at him, thinking him mad, but eventually relented and asked him to name a suitable bride.
The herdsman chose the maiden who transformed into a she-dog as his bride, and the old man accepted a bride price for her.
The wedding took place with great fanfare, and the newlywed couple returned to the herdsman's home.
Every night, as his husband slept, the maiden would slip out of the dog skin and roam free.
The herdsman discovered his wife's secret and snatched the dog skin from her grasp, burning it in the fire to prevent her transformation.
News of the herdsman's good fortune spread throughout the village, and people came to congratulate him on his wisdom in marrying a dog.
Jitu, an envious friend, tried to follow the herdsman's example by wooing a she-dog for himself.
Despite warnings signs, Jitu's party forcibly applied oil and turmeric to his bride, who broke free and gave chase.
Jitu was left with shame and humiliation, and eventually took his own life in a fit of despair.
A monk was seated on the seashore and saw a kite fly holding a little mouse.
The mouse escaped and fell onto the monk.
Touched by compassion, the monk took the mouse home with him.
He prayed to change the mouse into a young girl, which was immediately granted.
The monk led the young girl to his wife and asked her to treat her as their own child.
When the young girl reached womanhood, the monk asked her to choose a husband.
She replied that she wanted a husband who was the most powerful in the world.
The monk suggested the sun as a potential husband, but it said there was someone more powerful than itself.
The sun referred the monk to the cloud, which obscured its rays and made them pale.
The cloud in turn referred the monk to the wind, which drove it from east to west.
The wind said that there was someone more powerful than itself, namely the mountain.
The mountain replied that there was something even more powerful, namely the rat that bored into its sides.
The monk asked the rat if it wanted to marry his daughter, but it refused due to their incompatibility.
The monk prayed to change his daughter back into a mouse, which she was immediately transformed into, and she ran away with the rat.
A wise and compassionate monk named Kaito meditated on the seashore.
A kite flew by with a tiny mouse clinging to its tail.
The mouse fell from the sky and landed at Kaito's feet.
Kaito picked up the mouse and wrapped it in a large leaf.
Kaito prayed to the gods to transform the mouse into a young girl.
The mouse was transformed into a beautiful maiden.
Kaito's wife welcomed the young girl into their home and treated her as one of their own.
When the young girl reached womanhood, Kaito said it was time for her to marry.
The young girl desired a husband who was the most powerful in all the land.
Kaito sought out the sun as a potential suitor for his daughter.
The sun replied that it was not the most powerful and recommended the cloud as a suitor.
Kaito sought out the cloud, which recommended the wind as a suitor.
The wind recommended the mountain as a suitor.
The mountain recommended the rat as a suitor, and Kaito was able to reunite his daughter with her true love.
A devout monk sat on the seashore, his heart filled with compassion for all living creatures.
A kite soared overhead, carrying a tiny mouse in its talons.
The mouse escaped and fell into the monk's lap.
Moved by pity, the monk gently wrapped the mouse in a leaf and brought it back to his hermitage.
Fearful that his brethren might not be inclined to care for the little creature, he prayed to the Almighty to transform it into a young maiden.
The mouse was transformed into a beautiful young girl, endowed with radiant beauty and charm.
The monk presented the young woman to his wife, saying, 'Behold, my dear sister, this child who belongs to me. I beseech you to treat her as our own daughter.'

As she grew in age and wisdom, the monk approached her and said, 'My dear child, thou art now a grown woman, and it is time for thee to marry. Choose thy husband from among the mighty ones of this world, that I may unite thee with him.'
The young woman replied, 'Since you give me leave to choose, I desire a husband who is the most powerful in all the land.'
The monk's eyes widened in surprise, and he said, 'Perhaps thou wouldst have the great Sun as thy husband?'
He set forth to find the Sun, and when he arrived at its radiant throne, he asked if it would be willing to marry the young woman.
The Sun replied, 'I am indeed mighty, but I know of one who is more powerful than I. It is the Cloud that obscures my light and dulls my warmth.'
The monk sought out the Cloud, which said, 'And I, in turn, know of one who is mightier than I. Go find the Wind, which drives me from east to west.'
Finally, the monk came before the Mountain, which said, 'I too have met my match in a creature so small and insignificant that it bores into me and makes its home within my depths.'
The Bodhisatta came to life as a young lion.
A certain hare lived beneath a palm sapling at the foot of a vilva tree near the Western Ocean.
The hare thought, 'If this earth should be destroyed, what would become of me?'
A ripe vilva fruit fell on a palm leaf, and the hare fled thinking the earth was collapsing.
Another hare saw him running and asked the cause of his panic flight.
The first hare told the second hare that the earth was breaking up without looking back.
One hundred thousand hares took to flight together, followed by other animals such as deer, boar, elk, buffalo, wild ox, rhinoceros, tiger, and elephant.
The Bodhisatta saw the headlong flight of the animals and thought they were misunderstanding a sound.
The Bodhisatta got before them to the foot of a mountain and roared three times to stop their flight.
The lion asked the elephants if they knew about the earth collapsing, but they said no.
The Bodhisatta questioned each animal group in turn until he reached the hares, who pointed to one particular hare as the source of the information.
The Bodhisatta asked the suspected hare if it was true that the earth was breaking up, and the hare said yes.
The Bodhisatta took the hare to the palm grove and had him show the place where he claimed to have seen the earth collapsing.
The lion discovered that a ripe vilva fruit had fallen on a palm leaf, causing the sound that led to the panic flight.
A young lion emerged from his slumber, born of the Bodhisatta's spirit.
He roamed the forest, strong and fierce, with eyes like shining stars on a clear night.
Near the Western Ocean, a humble hare lived beneath a vilva tree.
A ripe vilva fruit fell onto a palm leaf with a loud thud, causing the hare to flee in terror.
The hare's companion asked him why he fled, but he refused to explain.
The second hare pressed for an explanation, and the first hare finally revealed that 'the earth is collapsing'.
A great host of animals joined the chase, driven by fear of the earth's collapse.
The Bodhisatta realized something was amiss and sprang forward to stop the fleeing animals.
He roared three times, causing the animals to halt in their tracks.
The Bodhisatta asked why they fled, but they claimed it was due to a misinterpreted sound.
The deer finally admitted that they didn't know, and the hares were accused of starting the panic.
The Bodhisatta questioned the accused hare about his claim, who explained how he misinterpreted a falling vilva fruit as the earth's collapse.
The Bodhisatta took the hare with him to investigate and found that it was just a ripe vilva fruit that had fallen onto a palm leaf.
He reassured the animals that the earth was not crumbling and they were finally free from fear.
A young lion, born from the earth itself, roamed the forests as a mighty beast.
Near the Western Ocean, a hare made his home beneath the shade of a young palm sapling at the foot of a venerable vilva tree.
A ripe vilva fruit fell from above and landed with a loud thud on a palm leaf.
The hare's heart skipped a beat as he leapt to his feet, convinced that the earth was indeed collapsing.
Without looking back, he fled in terror, racing across the land with all haste.
More hares joined the chase, until a hundred thousand of them were racing across the land together.
The procession stretched for leagues, a mighty host of animals fleeing from some unknown calamity.
The Bodhisatta beheld this spectacle with sorrow in his heart and realized that the earth was not crumbling beneath their feet.
He strode forward to confront the fleeing beasts and asked why they fled with such haste.
The Bodhisatta turned his attention to the hare who had started this chain of events and gently scolded him for jumping to conclusions.
He realized that the sound was merely the thud of the falling fruit on the palm leaf, and not a sign of the earth's collapse.
The Bodhisatta reassured the herd and let them return to their homes, thanks to his wisdom and compassion.
A farmer's wife was winnowing corn.
A crow swooped off with a grain from the winnowing basket and perched on a tree close by to eat it.
The farmer's wife flung a clod at the bird with so good an aim that the crow fell to the ground, dropping the grain of corn.
The grain of corn rolled into a crack in the tree.
The farmer's wife ran up to the crow and seized it by the tail.
The crow promised to return the grain of corn to avoid being killed.
However, the crow could not reach the grain in the crack with his beak or claws.
The crow flew off to a woodman and asked him to cut down the tree.
The woodman refused to cut down the tree.
The crow then flew on to the king's palace and asked for his help.
The king refused to kill the man who was responsible for cutting down the tree.
The crow then flew on to the queen and asked her to coax the king into helping him.
The queen refused to coax the king.
The crow continued his journey until he met a cat who was eager to catch a mouse that he had mentioned.
Akua was winnowing corn on the outskirts of the village.
A crow swooped down and snatched a grain from Akua's basket.
The crow perched on a nearby tree, its black feathers glistening like polished ebony.
Akua hurled a clod at the crow with such precision that it fell to the ground.
The grain dropped into a narrow crack in the tree, out of reach for the crow.
The crow flew off to seek help from the wise elder of the village.
The wise elder refused to intervene on behalf of the crow.
The crow continued on to the palace of the king, who sat like a mighty baobab tree.
The king sent the crow to the queen, who smiled sweetly and declined to help.
The crow met a snake in the forest, which refused to bite the queen.
The crow encountered a stick, which refused to beat the snake.
A fire refused to burn the stick, and water refused to quench its flames.
An ox refused to drink from the water, and a rope refused to bind the ox.
The crow met a mouse, who laughed at the idea of gnawing through the rope.
A farmer's wife toiled away at her winnowing basket, separating grain from chaff.
A cunning crow swooped in, snatching a single grain and perching on a nearby tree to devour it.
The farmer's wife hurled a clod of earth with such precision that the crow tumbled to the ground, dropping its prize into a narrow crack in the trunk.
The wretched bird promised to return the grain but was unable to retrieve it from the crevice.
The crow flew off to seek aid from a woodcutter, who refused to lend his axe to the bird's cause.
The crow winged its way to the palace of the king, beseeching him to dispatch the man who had thwarted the crow's plans.
The monarch sent the crow packing with a stern refusal.
The crow then flew on to the queen's chambers, begging her to coax the king into compliance, but she too refused.
The crow met a serpent who declined to bite the queen.
A stick that refused to strike the snake was encountered by the crow next.
The crow then encountered a fire that refused to consume the stick.
Water that refused to quench the flames was met by the crow's quest.
An ox politely declined to drink from the water, and the crow continued its journey.
A rope that wouldn't bind the ox was encountered by the crow next.
Brer Rabbit was going along through the bushes singing to himself.
He saw a shadow pass before him.
He looked up, and there was Mr. Hawk sailing around and around.
Every time he sailed around, he got a little closer,
but Brer Rabbit didn't notice this,
and by and by, down he dropped right slam-bang on Brer Rabbit.
Mr. Hawk held him in a mighty tight grip.
He held him so tight that it made Brer Rabbit's breath come short like it does off a long journey.
Brer Rabbit hollered and he begged,
but that didn't do any good.
Then Brer Rabbit lay still and studied about what in the name of goodness he was going to do.
Brer Rabbit up and allowed, 'I don't know what you want with me, Mr. Hawk.'
Mr. Hawk said, 'I'll do away with you, and then I'll go catch me a couple of jay-birds.'
A cunning rabbit named Chonguita was strolling along in a bamboo forest, singing to himself as he picked wild berries for dinner.
A shadow flitted across the path ahead, and Chonguita looked up to see a majestic hawk soaring overhead.
The hawk, Mr. Kaito, circled lower and lower, his piercing gaze fixed on the rabbit below.
Down swooped Mr. Kaito, talons extended, and Chonguita felt himself lifted from the ground.
The hawk's grip was like a vice, making it hard for Chonguita to breathe.
Chonguita lay still, thinking quickly about how to escape his predicament.
He said with a humble tone, 'I'm not worth eating, Mr. Kaito.'
Mr. Kaito scoffed at the idea, saying, 'No, no! I'll make short work of you and then go catch those pesky jay-birds that have been stealing food from my nest.'
Chonguita begged Mr. Kaito to catch the jay-birds first, saying, 'Please, sir, do take care of those birds! They're a nuisance, and I'd rather stay here until you return.'
Mr. Kaito saw through Chonguita's ruse, saying, 'Ah, Brer Rabbit, you've fooled many before, but not me!'
Chonguita proposed another plan, saying, 'If you can't catch the jay-birds now, perhaps it would be better to wait until I'm tamer and less wild.'
Mr. Kaito's expression changed, and he seemed to consider this idea.
Chonguita pressed his advantage, saying, 'Or maybe it would be best if you waited for me to grow bigger and more plump?'
Mr. Kaito's eyes lit up at the prospect of a full meal.
Brer Rabbit strolled along, whistling a merry tune, when he chanced upon a shadow dancing across the ground before him.
He looked up to behold Mr. Hawk soaring overhead, his sharp eyes scanning the landscape below.
As the hawk flew closer, Brer Rabbit failed to notice, and soon enough, down swooped the bird, pinning the rabbit beneath its talons.
Mr. Hawk held fast, his grip like a vice, making it hard for Brer Rabbit to breathe.
The rabbit squirmed and struggled, but to no avail.
He hollered and begged, but Mr. Hawk remained unmoved.
Finally, Brer Rabbit lay still, pondering his predicament.
Mr. Hawk said he would first catch some jay-birds, as they were far more succulent than the rabbit.
Brer Rabbit begged Mr. Hawk to spare the jay-birds, promising to wait patiently until he returned.
Mr. Hawk was not fooled by Brer Rabbit's words and decided to release him instead.
The next day, Chonguita, a clever and agile young woman, chanced upon Brer Rabbit as he strolled through the forest.
She challenged him to a series of tests: whoever won each challenge would claim victory.
Chonguita emerged victorious in all three challenges, including wearing the finest coat and most elegant cap.
Brer Rabbit slunk away, defeated once more by his own ineptitude.
An old man was returning home laden with firewood when he was overtaken by a terrible thunderstorm.
He sought shelter in a hollow tree.
After the storm had abated, the old man heard a sound of merriment close at hand.
He peeped out from his place of retreat and saw demons dancing and singing and drinking.
The old man began to laugh at their strange dancing and eventually left the tree to watch them better.
A demon was dancing by himself, and the chief of the company was displeased with his antics.
The leader of the demons asked if anyone could dance better than the clumsy demon.
The old man offered to show his skill as a dancer, having been one at some point in his youth.
The old man danced before the demons, who congratulated him and asked for more performances.
The chief of the demons asked the old man to dance again the following night and offered to remove a wen from his cheek as a pledge.
The chief removed the wen without causing pain, and then he and his companions vanished.
The next day, the old man's wife was pleased with his good fortune in losing the wen.
A wicked old man living next door heard about the old man's encounter with the demons and decided to try to deceive them.
The wicked old man hid in the hollow tree and attempted to dance for the demons, but they were displeased with his performance and returned the wen to him.
An elderly man returned home from gathering firewood under a fierce thunderstorm.
He stumbled upon a hollow tree and sought refuge inside.
A group of demons were dancing and singing within the hollow tree.
The leader of the demons asked if anyone could dance better than one clumsy demon.
The elderly man offered to show off his dancing skills.
He began to dance before the demons and they cheered and clapped in delight.
The leader of the demons offered him a cup of sak and asked him to dance again.
The old man danced with abandon and the demons roared with laughter.
The leader approached him and asked for a token of his visit, removing a wen from his cheek.
The news of his encounter spread through the village, and soon his neighbor heard about it.
The wicked old man begged his neighbor to tell him more about the hollow tree and the demons.
He decided to try his luck with the demons, hiding in the hollow tree at sunset.
His clumsy dancing was met with jeers from the demons, who flung a wen back onto his cheek as punishment.
An elderly woodsman was beset by a tempest's fury as he trudged homeward, burdened with faggots for the fire.
He stumbled upon a hollow tree, its ancient bark twisted and gnarled with age.
A company of demons capered and danced beneath the moon's silver rays.
The woodsman burst into laughter at their antics and found himself standing among them.
One demon danced with a clumsiness that brought a smile to the woodsman's lips.
The leader of the demons demanded someone to dance better than the clumsy demon.
The woodsman offered to demonstrate his skills before the demons and danced beneath the moon's watchful gaze.
The demons applauded and cheered, offering him a cup of enchanted sak that granted him strength and vitality.
The leader of the demons approached the woodsman with a mischievous glint in his eye and asked for a token of his visit.
The demon removed the wen on the woodsman's right cheek as a pledge, and the demons vanished with it.
The woodsman stumbled homeward, still feeling the warmth of their applause.
A wicked old man, who dwelled in a neighboring cottage, was enraged by the woodsman's success and called upon him with feigned friendship and curiosity.
The wicked old man crept into the hollow tree to watch the demons dance and was seized by a sudden urge to join in.
The demons flung the wen at the old man's right cheek, where it remained firmly fixed.
A certain man was overtaken by darkness among the mountains.
The man sought shelter in the trunk of a hollow tree.
A large company of elves assembled at the place where the man was hiding.
The man peeped out from his hiding place and was frightened by the elves.
However, the elves began to feast and drink wine, and to amuse themselves by singing and dancing.
The man, caught by the infection of the fun, forgot his fright and crept out of his hollow tree to join in the revels.
When the day was about to dawn, the elves said to the man that he must come out and have a dance with them again.
The elves took a large wen that grew on the man's forehead as pawn and left the place.
The man walked off to his own house in high glee at having passed a jovial night.
A neighbor of the man heard about his luck and was smitten with envy.
The envious neighbor went off to hunt for the hollow tree where he spent the night.
Elves mistook the neighbor for their former boon-companion and said they would give him back his pledge.
One of the elves stuck the pawned wen onto the neighbor's forehead on top of the other wen he already had.
A man sought refuge in the hollow trunk of an ancient tree.
The tree was home to a group of mischievous spirits known as the Yoruba Orishas.
The Orishas descended upon the tree and invited the man to join in their merriment.
The man danced with the Orishas under the starry sky until dawn began to break.
The Orishas bid the man farewell but took a token from his forehead – a wen that had plagued him for years.
The man returned home with a heart full of cheer and a face free from the wen's burden.
He shared his tale with his friends and family, who rejoiced at his good fortune.
An envious neighbor listened to the man's story with growing unease due to his own suffering from a wen.
The envious neighbor set out to find the ancient tree, determined to claim its magic for himself.
The Orishas welcomed the neighbor with open arms, thinking him to be their former companion.
One of the Orishas pulled out the pawned wen and stuck it onto the man's forehead on top of his existing wen.
The envious neighbor returned home in tears, realizing that his covetous heart had led him astray.
The villagers gathered to hear the tale and nodded in agreement with their elder's wise words.
A certain man found himself forced to seek refuge in the hollow trunk of an ancient tree.
A band of mischievous elves gathered 'neath the branches, their laughter and music carrying on the night air.
The man's initial fright gave way to curiosity as the elves' revelry continued unabated.
He crept from his hiding place to join in their festivities.
The elves welcomed him with open arms, and together they danced beneath the starry sky until the first light of dawn.
The elves presented the man with a challenge: to prove himself worthy of their company, he must compete against them in three trials.
He accepted the challenge and proved himself to be the equal of the elves in each trial.
The elves declared him the winner of their games and gifted him with a magical token that would bring him good fortune for all eternity.
A neighboring villager, consumed by envy and discontent, sought to replicate the man's luck for himself.
The envious neighbor convinced the elves to return his 'pledge' under false pretenses.
One of the elves presented the villager with a wen that grew on his forehead – only to stick it onto the top of another wen he already bore.
The envious neighbor returned home in tears, his envy and discontent now matched by the physical burden of two wens.
A vessel from Batavia was on the point of entering Table Bay in stress of weather during the Dutch occupation of the Cape.
The batteries fired on the distressed ship and compelled it to put to sea, where it was lost.
According to sailors, the vessel has continued to beat about ever since and will continue to do so till the day of judgment.
The 'Dutchman' is said to appear generally to ships in a heavy gale with all sail set.
On April 6, 1823, His Majesty's ship Leven saw her consort the Barracouta about two miles to leeward when off Point Danger.
The peculiar rig of the Barracouta left no doubt as to her identity, and many well-known faces were distinctly visible looking towards the Leven.
Capt. Owen attempted to close with the Barracouta to speak, but she stood away instead of joining the Leven.
The next morning, the Leven anchored in Simon's Bay where for a whole week the Barracouta was anxiously expected.
On her arrival (the 14th), it was seen by her log that she was 300 miles from the Leven when the latter thought she saw her.
The Leven witnessed another similar phenomenon where a boat was apparently lowered as if to lure its victim.
A veteran sailor on board the Leven was not caught by the 'Dutchman' and the ship reached England in safety.
As a passenger in a merchant ship, I saw a vessel in nearly similar circumstances three times.
The third time, we hoisted lights over the gangway to speak with the stranger, but it disappeared instantly.
A vessel from Batavia is beset by fate as it approaches Table Bay.
The Dutch occupation of the Cape has imposed a stern rule: no ship is allowed to enter the bay during the tempestuous season.
The vessel is forced back out to sea by batteries on the shore firing upon it.
Years go by, and many claim to have seen the ghostly ship, its sails billowing in the wind like a specter.
Captain W.F.W. Owen of His Majesty's ship Leven spots the Barracouta off Point Danger on April 6, 1823.
The Barracouta refuses to respond when Captain Owen attempts to hail it.
The Leven continues on its course, bound for Simon's Bay, while the Barracouta disappears into the distance.
Seven days later, the Barracouta docks in Simon's Bay, revealing that it had been 300 miles away from the Leven when it was spotted.
The Leven witnesses a similar phenomenon with a ghostly ship appearing on the horizon and vanishing into thin air.
A veteran sailor recounts his three encounters with the Flying Dutchman, each time in similar circumstances: a storm raging outside and the vessel struggling to stay afloat.
In the land of the Cape of Good Hope, a young woman named Chonguita lived, known for her unparalleled skills in navigation and seamanship.
A ship from Batavia struggled to enter Table Bay due to harsh winds and pounding waves.
The captain of the ship, Van der Meer, was forced to put out to sea once more, leaving behind his crew and passengers.
Years passed, and Chonguita grew into a skilled navigator in her own right, studying the tides, winds, and currents of the Cape.
Captain Van der Meer's ghostly vessel appeared to ships in distress, luring them to their doom with promises of safety and comfort.
Chonguita's reputation as a master navigator caught the attention of Captain Van der Meer, who challenged her to a series of competitions.
The first challenge took place on a stormy night, where Chonguita and Van der Meer tried to outguess each other in navigating their vessels through treacherous waters.
Chonguita emerged victorious in the first challenge, her mastery of the sea and its secrets leaving Van der Meer in awe.
The second challenge took place on a calm morning, where Chonguita and Van der Meer competed to see who could create the most effective sail.
Chonguita emerged victorious again in the second challenge, her mastery of sailing and navigation leaving Van der Meer in awe.
The final challenge took place on a stormy night, where Chonguita and Van der Meer competed to see who could create the most effective wall picture.
Chonguita emerged victorious for the third time in the final challenge, her mastery of art and navigation leaving Van der Meer in awe.
Captain Van der Meer conceded defeat, acknowledging Chonguita's superiority as a sailor and navigator.
From that day forward, the ghostly vessel was said to appear less frequently, its restless spirits no longer lured by the promise of revenge.
She said one day to the bad one: 'Go and draw a bucket of water.'
The bad one did not want to go, and so she would not obey her mother.
The good daughter, however, said: 'I will go and draw it.'
She went to draw the water, and the bucket fell down the well.
She said: 'If I go home now without the bucket, who knows what my mother will do to me?'
So she climbed down the well, and at the bottom found a narrow passage, with a door.
She knocked at the door. 'Have you not found a cord and bucket?'
There was a saint there, who answered: 'No, my child.'

The Madonna went away, and the good girl put the house in order, gave the child his broth, swept the house; and while she was sweeping, instead of finding dirt, she found coral and other beautiful things.
When the Madonna came back, she asked: 'Have you done all I told you to do?'
A mother with three daughters lived in a small hut not far from the village.
The mother sent her youngest daughter, Chonguita, to fetch water from the well, but the eldest sister, Akua, refused to obey their mother's command.
Chonguita took responsibility and went to draw the water, but it slipped from her hands and fell down the well.
Chonguita climbed down into the well to retrieve the bucket.
At the bottom of the well, Chonguita found a narrow passage with three doors.
She knocked on the first door and asked if they had seen her bucket, but the wise old saint replied 'No, child'.
Chonguita continued to the second door and found the devil, who answered with anger in his voice.
She knocked on the third door and found the Madonna, who said 'Yes, my child. I have seen your bucket'.
The Madonna asked Chonguita to take care of her little son, give him his soup, sweep the house, and put everything in order while she was away.
Chonguita took on the tasks and found beautiful coral and other treasures instead of dirt when sweeping the floor.
When the Madonna returned, Chonguita replied that she had done as told, but also kept the treasures she found.
The Madonna smiled and said 'Very well, keep them for yourself' and gave Chonguita a choice between two dresses – one of calico and one of silk.
Chonguita chose the calico dress, but the Madonna gave her the silk one.
Akua, the eldest sister, let the bucket fall down the well and climbed down to retrieve it, but did not follow the Madonna's instructions when she encountered her.
Two sisters lived in a small village nestled in the mountains, one virtuous as a lotus flower and the other as wicked as a serpent.
The virtuous sister said she would go to fetch water from the well, but her wicked sister refused to obey their mother's command.
The virtuous sister went to draw the water, but the bucket fell into the well.
She climbed down the well, determined not to return home without the bucket.
At the bottom of the well, she found a narrow passage leading to three doors.
The virtuous sister knocked on the first door and asked if anyone had seen her cord and bucket.
A wise old sage replied that he hadn't seen them, so she continued on her journey.
She knocked on the second door and again asked about her cord and bucket, but it was the devil who answered this time.
The virtuous sister didn't falter and moved on to the third door, where she met the benevolent Goddess of Mercy.
The Goddess told her that she had a young son who needed his soup, so she gave it to him instead.
She swept the house and found coral and treasures instead of dirt.
The Goddess returned and asked if she had done as instructed, but the virtuous sister kept the treasures for herself.
The Goddess surprised her by giving her a silk dress instead of calico, and a silver thimble instead of brass.
She gave her the bucket and cord, telling her to look up at the sky when she left the passage.
A brilliant star fell upon her forehead as she looked up, and she returned home to find her mother waiting for her.
The wicked sister watched from afar and grew envious of her sister's good fortune.
She climbed down the well and knocked on each door in turn, but was tempted by the devil's words at the Goddess's door.
The wicked sister ate the broth meant for the young son and lied to the Goddess when she returned.
She chose the wrong dress and was given a brass thimble instead of silver, with a lump of dirt falling on her forehead as she left.
The virtuous sister's mother saw the contrast between her two daughters and understood that the Goddess had intervened to teach them a lesson.
A Sultan had a daughter, and the daughter used to be taught the Koran.
The Sultan went on a pilgrimage, and entrusted his daughter to a priest and said, 'Continue to teach that girl the Koran.'
The priest coveted the girl, wishing to lie with her, but the girl refused.
One day she said, 'Come to me tomorrow.'
On the day arranged she removed from the house the ladder by which the priest used to ascend.
He then sent a letter to her father, and he wrote, 'Your daughter has become a whore.'
The Sultan returned from the pilgrimage, and he was angry with the girl, and he handed her over to some slaves,
and they took the girl, and brought her to a wooded place, and they cut off her legs while they dug her grave.
While they were digging the grave she crawled away, and went into some trees and hid.
A caravan passed by the place and camped where the girl lay.
In the afternoon as the party were loading up the camels, they saw the girl sitting under a tree.
The Sultan's son saw the girl's face, and he said to the man whose house she dwelt in, 'Let me marry that girl from you.'
She bore two children, and while she was with child the young man said, 'I am going on a pilgrimage'.
A mighty Sultan had a daughter as lovely as a lotus flower.
The Sultan entrusted his daughter to a wise and venerable priest to teach her the sacred teachings of the Koran.
The priest harbored impure intentions towards the young girl.
She refused his advances with a firmness that would put a mountain to shame.
One day, she said to him, 'Come to me tomorrow,' and when he arrived, she cleverly removed the ladder by which he used to ascend to her chambers.
The priest sent a deceitful letter to the Sultan, accusing his daughter of being a wanton woman.
The Sultan returned from his pilgrimage in a fury, intending to have his daughter's throat cut by some slaves.
While the slaves dug her grave, she crawled away using her cunning and agility to evade their blades.
A caravan happened upon the wooded place where the girl lay hidden, and a kind-hearted man took pity on her and brought her to his home in a nearby town.
The Sultan's son chanced upon the girl's face and was struck by its beauty, requesting permission from the man who had taken her in to marry her.
A Sultan's daughter was as lovely as a rose in bloom.
She had been taught the sacred words of the Koran by a wise priest.
The priest began to covet the girl's virtue, but his affection soon turned to lust.
The Sultan set out on a long pilgrimage, entrusting his daughter to the priest's care.
The girl refused the priest's advances and devised a plan to thwart him.
She removed the ladder from the house, preventing the priest from reaching her chambers.
The priest sent a letter to the Sultan, falsely accusing his daughter of being immoral.
The Sultan believed the priest's words and ordered his daughter to be handed over to ruthless slaves.
The slaves took the girl to a secluded wooded place and began to dig her grave.
The girl managed to crawl away and hide among the trees, while the slaves finished digging her grave.
The slaves brought back a bottle filled with gazelle blood, claiming it was the girl's lifeblood.
A caravan passed by the wooded place, and one of the travelers spotted the girl sitting under a tree.
The Sultan's son chanced upon the girl and fell in love with her beauty.
The girl married the young prince and bore him two children, but later regained her legs after having a wondrous dream.
A peasant and his wife had a child they would not baptize until they could find a just man for his godfather.
The father took the child in his arms and went into the street to look for this just man.
He met our Lord, who was walking along, and asked if he was a just person.
Our Lord replied that he did not know whether he was just.
The peasant passed on and met the Madonna, asking her if she was a just person.
The Madonna said she did not know, but told him to keep looking for someone who was just.
He continued his search and met Death, who claimed to be a just person.
Death agreed to baptize the child with the peasant.
After baptism, Death led the peasant into a long room filled with many burning lights.
The lights were revealed to be the souls of all people in the world, and Death showed the peasant his own light was about to expire.
The peasant asked what would happen when his oil ran out, and Death replied that he would have to come with him.
The peasant begged for a small amount of oil from the child's lamp to put in his own, but Death refused.
Death told the peasant that he had found a just person and now it was time for him to go home and prepare for death.
A humble farmer and his wife had a child, but they needed to find a worthy godparent to guide him on his life's journey.
The father took the child in his arms and set out into the village square to seek someone who embodied justice and righteousness.
He encountered a stranger whose presence seemed both familiar and mysterious.
The farmer asked the stranger to be his child's godfather, but the stranger replied that one cannot truly know if they are just.
The farmer continued his search for a worthy godparent.
He met a wise woman who was revered throughout the land for her compassion and kindness.
The wise woman told the farmer that he would find one who is just, but she did not claim to be so herself.
The farmer met another woman whose presence seemed both gentle and unyielding, and she claimed to be just.
She agreed to baptize the child, and afterwards showed the farmer what it means to be just.
Death led the peasant into a vast chamber filled with an abundance of light.
The peasant beheld the radiance that surrounded him and saw his own light among them.
He asked Death when his oil would run out, and what would become of him then.
Death told the peasant that he must come with her when his oil runs out, as she guides all souls to their final rest.
The peasant implored Death to let him take some of his son's oil, but she refused and told him to go home and arrange his affairs.
A humble farmer and his wife had given birth to a precious child.
They sought a wise and virtuous godparent to bless their little one with the sacred rites of passage.
The father cradled the infant in his arms and ventured into the village to find this noble soul.
He encountered a venerable sage, who was none other than the revered Master Lao.
Master Lao replied, 'Ah, but I am not one to judge myself worthy of such an honor.'
The farmer bowed his head in respect and continued on his journey.
He met a gentle woman with a kind smile, who was none other than the revered Lady Meng Jiangnu.
Lady Meng Jiangnu replied, 'Alas, I am not one to claim such a title. But go on, you will find someone who embodies justice and compassion.'
The farmer encountered a mysterious woman with a somber aura, who was none other than the enigmatic Jiangshi.
Jiangshi nodded solemnly and said, 'Indeed, I am just! Let us perform the sacred rites of passage for your child.'
A little girl named Katie was passin' by an old woman's house.
The old woman opened the door and told Katie to come in.
Katie saw a beautiful room with lots of pretty pictures inside.
The old woman offered Katie some apples to eat.
After eating the apples, the old woman told Katie to stay with her all the time.
Katie remembered that she had an errand to get something for supper from her mother.
The old woman offered Katie anything she wanted in exchange for staying with her.
The old woman used a wand to make lots of good things appear at Katie's mother's house.
Katie agreed to stay with the old woman for a week.
At the end of the week, Katie had a dream about an elf warning her that the witch would kill her.
The next night, Katie had the same dream again.
The elf left Katie a comb and a handkerchief with instructions to drop them when the witch came after her.
Katie jumped out of the window in the middle of the night to escape the old woman.
As she was nearin' home, Katie dropped her comb and a forest grew up, blocking the witch's path.
In a small village nestled in the mountains, there lived an elderly woman named Obaachan, who was feared and avoided by the villagers for her mysterious ways.
A young girl named Chikako wandered into Obaachan's humble abode, where she found a beautifully decorated room filled with exquisite calligraphy and paintings.
Obaachan welcomed Chikako warmly and offered her delicious rice cakes to eat.
After finishing her snack, Obaachan asked Chikako to stay with her for a while, but the girl remembered that her mother had sent her on an errand to fetch some ingredients for dinner.
However, Obaachan insisted that Chikako could have anything she desired, and with a wave of her staff, a magnificent coat appeared before the girl.
Chikako was amazed by the coat's beauty but knew it wouldn't fit her. She politely declined again, but Obaachan was insistent.
She conjured up a hat to match the coat, but Chikako pointed out that it didn't suit her either.
Undeterred, Obaachan created a stunning wall painting of the mountains and forests surrounding their village.
Chikako realized that Obaachan's powers were indeed extraordinary, but she still had to complete her errand for her mother. She promised to return soon,
but as the days passed, Chikako began to have ominous dreams.
In her visions, a wise spirit warned her of Obaachan's true intentions and instructed her to drop a comb and handkerchief when the time came.
That night, Chikako fled from Obaachan's house through the window and dropped the comb behind her.
A towering bamboo grove sprouted up, blocking Obaachan's path.
As she struggled to navigate the dense foliage, Chikako dropped the handkerchief, and a mighty river burst forth, forcing Obaachan to cross its waters.
In a small village nestled in the rolling hills of the countryside, there lived an old crone who was feared and reviled by all who knew her.
One day, as a young maiden named Chonguita passed by the crone's humble abode, the old woman beckoned her to enter.
Chonguita, curious and innocent, stepped inside and found herself in a room filled with exquisite tapestries and paintings.
The crone offered her a basket of juicy apples, which Chonguita devoured with relish.
But Chonguita's mother had entrusted her with an errand to procure some provisions for supper.
The crone, however, seemed reluctant to let her go, and with a flick of her wand, the table was filled with an assortment of delicacies from Chonguita's own home.
Chonguita agreed to stay with the crone for a week, and as the days passed, she began to experience strange and vivid dreams.
In each dream, a mischievous sprite appeared to warn her that the crone intended to do her harm.
The sprite left her a comb and a handkerchief, instructing her to drop them when the crone made her escape.
One fateful night, Chonguita fled through the window and dropped the comb behind her.
A sturdy oak tree sprouted forth from the earth, its branches entwining the crone's path.
Undeterred, she pressed on, only to be confronted by a river that flowed from the handkerchief, forcing her to wade across.
As Chonguita neared her home, she saw the crone approaching, but just as they were about to meet, a woodsman appeared and struck off the crone's head with his axe.
He escorted Chonguita back to her relieved mother, who scolded her for disobeying her instructions in the first place.
Brer Wolf went into the house while Brer Rabbit and his mother were out picking salad for dinner.
Brer Rabbit returned home and noticed that everything was still, which made him feel suspicious.
He saw that the front door was slightly ajar, which he knew his mother and children would never leave open.
Brer Rabbit cautiously approached the house, stepping softly like a battercake.
He peeped through the windows and chimney corner but saw or heard nothing out of the ordinary.
Brer Rabbit decided to use his wit to figure out who was inside the house without going in himself.
He climbed a nearby persimmon stump and began hollering 'Heyo, house! Why don't you holler back?'
The house remained silent, but Brer Wolf inside the house was getting restless.
Brer Rabbit continued to holler louder and louder, trying to get a response from the house.
Finally, Brer Wolf tried to mimic the sound of a house by hollering 'Heyo, yo'se'f!'
Brer Rabbit was amused and laughed at Brer Wolf's attempt to talk like a house.
Brer Rabbit teased Brer Wolf, saying he would have to stand in the rain for a long time before he could talk hoarse like a house.
Uncle Remus continued the story, saying that after this incident, Brer Wolf slinked out of the house and made a break for home.
Old Master Rabbit and his young son were out foraging for bamboo shoots in Bamboo Grove.
Brer Wolf had slipped into their humble abode while they were away.
Master Rabbit arrived home with haste as the sun began to set.
He noticed that everything seemed eerily still and silent, making him feel uneasy.
The front door was slightly ajar, which was unusual since his family always ensured it was securely shut.
Master Rabbit approached cautiously, peering through the crack of the door but saw nothing out of the ordinary.
He listened intently, but heard no signs of life within, and pondered what to do next.
Master Rabbit decided to reason with whoever was hiding behind the door without confronting them directly.
He called out from a nearby bamboo stump, 'Heyo, House! Why don't you respond?'
Brer Wolf grew increasingly agitated and unsure of what to make of Master Rabbit's strange behavior.
Master Rabbit continued to holler, 'Heyo, House! Is something amiss within you?'
Brer Wolf began to feel a chill run down his spine as he peered through the crack in the door but saw nothing.
Finally, Brer Wolf hollered back in a hoarse voice, 'Heyo, Yo'self!'
Master Rabbit chuckled and thought, 'Ah-ah, Brer Wolf, that won't do. You'll have to stand out in the rain for a long time before you can talk as rough as this old house!'
Brer Wolf waited patiently within the walls of Brer Rabbit's humble abode.
Brer Rabbit, accompanied by his gentle wife and their little ones, ventured out to gather the evening's sustenance.
Brer Rabbit returned home, his keen senses on high alert.
He noticed that the front door creaked ominously on its hinges.
With stealthy steps, Brer Rabbit approached the house, his eyes scanning every nook and cranny for any sign of disturbance.
Brer Rabbit peered through the crack beneath the door, but all he saw was darkness.
He climbed an adjacent simmon stump and began to holler at the top of his lungs, 'Heyo, house! Why don't you respond?'
The silence from within the house only fueled Brer Wolf's confusion and unease.
Brer Rabbit's laughter echoed through the forest, a sound that sent Brer Wolf scurrying for cover.
With a final, exasperated cry, 'Heyo, house! Why don't you talk hoarse like you've got a bad cold?' Brer Wolf replied in a gruff voice, 'Heyo, yo'self!'
Brer Rabbit's chuckles turned into guffaws as he realized the absurdity of the situation.
With a flourish, he leapt from the simmon stump and landed with a thud on the forest floor.
The commotion was too much for Brer Wolf, who slunk away, defeated.
A man went into the forest to gather wood.
He saw a snake crushed under a large stone.
The man raised the stone with his axe and freed the snake.
The snake said it would eat the man in return for saving its life.
The man suggested they seek a judge to decide their fate.
They met a thin horse tied to an oak tree and asked for his judgment.
The horse agreed that the snake should eat the man as he was ill-rewarded.
They then met a mulberry tree and asked for its judgment on the matter.
The tree also agreed that the snake should eat the man as it had been well-rewarded.
However, they later met the fox who was asked to judge in favor of the man.
The fox agreed to judge in favor of the man after seeing how he saved the snake's life.
The man tricked the snake by leaving it under the stone and pretending to leave it there.
The fox was tricked by the man who put dogs in a bag instead of hens as promised.
The dogs ate the fox, illustrating that evil is rewarded while good is punished.
A man ventured into the forest to gather firewood for his family's evening meal.
He chanced upon a serpent crushed beneath a massive stone and lifted it with his axe, freeing the snake.
The serpent gazed at the man with eyes that seemed to burn like embers and threatened to devour him in return for saving its life.
The man stood firm and suggested seeking the counsel of one who has witnessed the world's ways.
Their first encounter was with a gaunt horse tied to an ancient oak tree, which advised the serpent to devour the man as it would be a matter of balance.
The serpent then asked a mulberry tree for its counsel, and the tree agreed that devouring the man would be a noble deed.
The man took the cunning fox aside to plead on his behalf, and she listened intently before agreeing to speak in his favor.
Together, they returned to the spot where the serpent lay beneath the stone, but the man refused to let it go free.
The fox requested a bag of hens from the man as payment for her services, which he promised to deliver the next morning.
However, the man tricked the fox by filling the bag with snarling dogs instead of hens, and they devoured her in an instant.
The villagers would say that this tale serves as a reminder of the world's ways: that kindness goes unrewarded, while malice is well-remunerated.
A humble woodsman was gathering firewood in a bamboo forest when he stumbled upon a serpent crushed beneath a massive stone.
He carefully lifted the stone with his axe handle, freeing the snake from its predicament.
As the serpent slithered away, it hissed at the woodsman, 'I will devour you for your kindness.'
The woodsman replied, 'Patience, my friend. Let us first seek the counsel of a wise one to determine if I am worthy of punishment.'
Their search led them to a withered nag tied to an ancient oak tree.
The snake asked the nag, 'Is it just for me to devour this man who saved my life?'
The nag's voice trembled as he replied, 'Indeed, it is only right. I was once a proud steed...'
As they continued their journey, they came across a mulberry tree, its branches gnarled with age and its leaves long gone.
The snake asked the ancient tree if it was just to devour the woodsman, who had saved its life.
The tree's voice creaked with age as it replied, 'Yes, it is only right. I have given my master countless mulberries...'
Their search led them to a cunning fox, who listened intently as the woodsman pleaded his case.
The fox said, 'I must see the situation for myself before rendering judgment.'
They returned to the spot where the snake was crushed beneath the stone, but when they arrived, the woodsman merely pointed at the serpent and said, 'You will remain there, under that stone.'
The fox, in payment for her services, requested a bag of chickens from the woodsman.
A crocodile made his burrow in the embankment of a tank near a village.
The mud dried and became hard, so the crocodile was unable to get out of the hole.
He was going to die.
A man passed by on his way to fetch a midwife to attend to his wife.
The crocodile heard him and said, 'Save me by breaking up the earth, so that I may get out.'
The man broke up the earth and let him out.
As there was no water left in the tank, the man placed the crocodile on his shoulder and went to the edge of the river.
He then placed the crocodile into the water, but no sooner had he done so than the crocodile seized him by the arm and was about to eat him.
The man said, 'It is good. You may eat me, but first let us ask two or three persons as witnesses.'
They came to a kumbuk tree and the man asked its opinion about the crocodile eating him.
The kumbuk tree said, 'O crocodile, do not let that man go. There is no animal so wicked as that man.'
He went and asked a cow about the situation, who also advised the crocodile to eat the man.
After that he asked a jackal about it, but the jackal wanted to hear the whole story before making a decision.
The man placed the crocodile on his shoulder and carried him back to his home, where the jackal advised him to beat the crocodile until he was dead.
Kofi was on his way to fetch a midwife for his wife, who was in labor with their firstborn child.
He heard a faint cry for help from a crocodile stuck in its burrow due to the dried mud.
Kofi broke up the earth with his bare hands and freed the crocodile from its prison.
As they made their way to the river, Kofi realized that the tank was dry and the crocodile would perish if he didn't find water.
Kofi walked for hours with the crocodile on his shoulder until they reached the edge of the mighty River Niger.
The crocodile seized Kofi by the arm and prepared to devour him when he was placed in the water.
Kofi asked the crocodile why it wanted to eat him, and the crocodile replied that it was hungry but acknowledged Kofi's kindness.
Kofi suggested asking the witnesses of their village for their opinion on whether he should be eaten by the crocodile.
They visited a majestic kumbuk tree and asked its opinion, which was that the man who wanted to eat Kofi was wicked.
Next, they visited a cow who agreed with the tree's assessment and said that the crocodile was a thief.
Finally, they came to a wise old jackal who asked for the whole story before giving his opinion.
The jackal advised Kofi to take up a stick and beat the crocodile until it was dead because it was wicked and had no regard for life or property.
Kofi's fate was sealed, and he killed the crocodile as per the jackal's advice.
Kofi returned home to his wife, who had given birth to a beautiful baby boy, and they sat together under their hut.
A cunning crocodile had made its lair in the embankment beside a tranquil tank.
The mud dried and hardened, trapping the beast.
A traveler passed by on his way to fetch a midwife to attend to his wife's impending birth.
The crocodile called out to the man for rescue, 'Kind sir, save me from this predicament! Break up the earth and set me free!'
The traveler obliged and broke open the hardened mud, releasing the crocodile.
As he placed the beast on his shoulder to transport it to the nearby river, the crocodile's intentions turned treacherous.
The man asked, 'Why would you eat me after I saved your life?'
The crocodile replied, 'Indeed, I am grateful for your kindness, but hunger has consumed my heart. I must eat.'
The man proposed a condition: 'Very well, I shall accept my demise, but first, let us seek the opinions of two or three witnesses.'
Together, they journeyed to gather the testimony of various creatures.
The first witness was a majestic kumbuk tree, who testified that the man was a thief and often pilfered its bark and leaves.
The next witness was a gentle cow, whose milk was often taken by the man without compensation or gratitude.
Finally, they came upon a wise jackal, who listened intently to the tale before rendering its verdict.
The jackal's demeanor changed from curiosity to indignation as it observed the man's wickedness and advised the traveler to strike down the crocodile.
A lion was trapped in a rocky cleft.
The lion asked a horse for help to be freed.
The horse agreed to help the lion on condition that he would not eat him.
The horse dug with his hooves until the lion was free.
As soon as the lion was free, he said he wanted to eat the horse.
The horse reminded the lion of their agreement not to eat each other.
The lion refused to honor his promise and threatened to eat the horse anyway.
The horse suggested going before a judge to settle the dispute.
The lion agreed to this and chose the fox as their judge.
They went to the rocky cleft where the lion was originally trapped.
The fox asked the lion to reenact how he was standing when he first asked for help.
The lion tried to reenact his original position but kept getting it wrong.
The fox told the lion to push himself in further until he got stuck again.
The horse then threw stones at the trapped lion until he was dead.
In a land far and wide, where the sun dipped into the horizon like a fiery blade, there lived a cunning lion with eyes as sharp as the morning dew.
He had grown tired of his life in the savannah, so he devised a plan to escape.
But alas, he was trapped in a rocky cleft, unable to free himself.
A wise and gentle horse, with a coat as black as the night sky, overheard the lion's plea for help.
The horse said, 'I'll do that,' but you must promise not to eat me.'
The lion promised to spare the horse's life.
The horse dug with his hooves until he had freed the lion.
But as soon as the lion was free, he forgot his promise and said, 'Now I am going to eat you!'
The horse replied, 'What were the conditions? Did we not agree that you would not eat me?'
They went to see the wise and cunning fox, who sat under a baobab tree, his eyes gleaming with wisdom.
The lion presented the question to the fox, but he refused to give a verdict until he had seen how the two of them were standing.
The fox told the lion to force himself back into the cleft, just as he was before.
The lion pushed and pushed until he was stuck fast, unable to get out again.
The horse threw stones at the lion until he was dead.
A cunning lion was trapped in a rocky cleft.
A wise and agile horse heard his cries for help and offered to free him.
The horse said, 'I'll do that, but you must promise not to devour me.'
The lion promised to spare the horse's life.
Once freed, however, the lion's true nature reasserted itself.
The lion roared at the horse, 'Now I will eat you!'
The horse stood firm and asked, 'Did we not agree that you would not devour me?'
The horse suggested they seek the judgment of a wise and impartial judge.
The fox was chosen as the arbiter due to his reputation for cunning.
The lion presented his case, claiming that his promise no longer bound him.
The fox proposed a test: to see how the two had stood in the rocky cleft when the horse first freed the lion.
With the fox's guidance, the lion was forced to reenact their original positions.
The fox declared that the lion was now exactly as he was before and could be freed again.
However, the horse had no intention of releasing the lion and began throwing stones at it until it was still and silent.
The farmer was terrified.
He unhitched one of the oxen and gave it to the lion.
The lion took it and dragged it away.
The farmer went home with the remaining ox and bought a second one that same evening so that the next day he would be able to plow again.

The next day the farmer plowed again from morning until evening, and when it was evening the lion came again and said, 'Farmer, give me one of your two oxen or I'll kill both of them and you as well.'
Again the farmer gave him an ox.
That evening he bought another ox so that he would be able to plow again the next day.
One evening the jackal came by as the farmer was driving his single ox home.
The jackal said, 'Every morning I see you leave the farmyard with two oxen and every evening I see you coming back with only one. Why is that?'
The farmer said, 'Every evening when I am finished with the day's work the lion comes and demands one of my oxen and threatens to kill me and both oxen if I don't grant his wish.'
The jackal said, 'If you promise to give me a sheep I will free you from the lion.'
The farmer answered, 'If you can free me from the lion I will gladly promise you a sheep.'

The jackal said, 'Tomorrow I will call out with a disguised voice from up there on the hill and ask who is speaking with you. Then you answer that it is only an Asko (a block of wood to be split). Have a hatchet at hand.'
The farmer killed the lion by splitting its skull with a hatchet.
A fierce lion arrived at Kaito's village, demanding one of his prized oxen every evening.
Kaito gave in to the lion's demands each time, purchasing another ox to replace the one taken.
A cunning jackal named Akira encountered Kaito and asked him why he seemed to have two oxen in the morning and only one at night.
Kaito confided in Akira about the lion's demands, and Akira offered to help him in exchange for a promise: if he freed Kaito from the lion's grasp, Kaito would reward him with a fine sheep.
Akira devised a plan to rid Kaito of the lion by pretending that an Asko (a sacred block of wood) was speaking to the lion when it arrived at dusk.
The next day, the lion appeared as expected, demanding one of Kaito's oxen.
Akira called out from a nearby hill, disguising his voice as the Asko's, and asked who spoke with Kaito.
The lion, trembling with fear, replied that it was the voice of the gods.
Kaito loudly declared the Asko to be nothing more than a simple block of wood and struck it with his hatchet.
The lion's skull was split by Kaito's hatchet, ending its reign of terror.
Akira descended from the hill, proud of their joint success, and claimed his reward: a fine ram that Kaito had promised him.
Kaito's wife suggested keeping the ram for themselves instead of giving it to Akira.
Akira was chased away by the snarling dog when he approached the wicker basket containing the ram.
Kaito and his family enjoyed the ram themselves, while Akira slunk away, a lesson learned about underestimating others' cunning.
A humble farmer named Giovanni lived in a small village nestled between two great rivers.
Giovanni's life was disrupted by a fearsome lion that demanded one of his oxen each night under threat of death.
The farmer complied with the lion's demands, sacrificing an ox each night and purchasing another in the evening market.
A cunning jackal named Lupino approached Giovanni one fateful eve and asked about the missing oxen.
Giovanni explained the lion's demands to Lupino, who devised a plan to free him from the beast's tyranny.
Lupino instructed Giovanni to respond that the lion was an Asko (a block of wood) when it arrived, and to strike at its skull with his hatchet.
Giovanni followed Lupino's plan, striking the lion's skull and shattering its body into two pieces.
Lupino claimed the fine ram that Giovanni owed him as payment for freeing him from the lion's tyranny.
Giovanni's wife packed the ram into a leather sack and instructed Giovanni to bring it back if Lupino did not take it.
When Lupino approached the basket with the ram, the dog leaped up and chased him away with a fierce bark.
Giovanni returned home to find the ram still untouched in the basket, and he decided to eat it himself instead of giving it to Lupino.
A king was rearing wild animals.
The king had learnt in a thorough manner the speech of animals.
The fowls were saying, 'Our king assists us very much; he gives us food and drink.'
The royal queen asked, 'What did you laugh at?'
The king said, 'I merely (nikan) laughed,' the king said.
The royal queen says, 'There is no one who laughs in that way without a reason.'
A cock says, 'His majesty our king is going to be lost. We don't want the food.'
The king having heard it, laughed at it also.
The queen said, 'Having caused the animals that are in this Lankawa (Ceylon) to be brought, let us build a tank.'
The king having gone with the animals, showed them a place [in which] to build a tank.
The jackal said, 'I looked whether in this country the female3s are in excess or the males are in excess.'
The king asked, 'By the account which thou knowest, are the females in excess or the males in excess?'
The jackal said, 'So far as I can perceive, the females are in excess in this country.'
The king called the jackal and said, 'Bola, males are in excess.'
A mighty king lived in a small village nestled between two great rivers who possessed a special gift – he could understand the language of animals.
The birds perched outside his window began to chatter among themselves, praising the king for providing for their needs and protecting them from harm.
The king overheard their conversation and couldn't help but chuckle at their praise.
His queen noticed her husband's amusement and asked him what had brought him such joy.
The king hesitated to reveal the secret of understanding animal language, knowing it would put his life in danger.
He finally decided to reveal the secret by announcing his intention to share their language with them in the courtyard.
A wise old cock spoke up, saying that they didn't want the king's charity and would be better off without him ruling over them.
The king laughed at the cock's bold words, but his queen was not amused and demanded an explanation for his amusement.
The king came up with a new excuse, saying he had an idea for building a grand tank in their kingdom.
He ordered the animals to be brought to the site where the tank would be built, accompanied by them and instructed them to begin construction.
As the days passed, it became clear that the animals were not capable of building a tank, resulting in chaos.
A clever jackal approached the king and offered an explanation for the animals' failure, pointing out the imbalance of nature in their kingdom.
The king was impressed by the jackal's wisdom and decided to investigate further, discovering that there were indeed more males than females in the kingdom.
The king realized his queen had been right all along – he did possess a special gift, one that allowed him to understand the complexities of nature.
A just and benevolent monarch lived in a far-off kingdom who had mastered the art of understanding the language of beasts.
The king overheard birds singing sweet melodies to their king, praising him for providing them with sustenance and shelter.
The queen inquired about the reason behind his merriment, but the king remained tight-lipped.
The queen pressed him for an explanation, threatening to take her leave or jump into a well if he didn't reveal the secret.
The king finally relented and decided to reveal the secret rather than see his wife harm herself.
A wise old cock spoke up, warning the king that he would meet his demise if he continued to provide for the animals.
The queen's suspicions were piqued once more when she saw her husband laugh again at the cock's words.
The king fabricated a tale about planning to construct a grand tank in honor of their kingdom to appease his wife.
The queen suggested gathering all the animals from Ceylon to build the tank together with her husband's idea.
As the days passed, it became apparent that the animals were merely pretending to work on the tank.
A sly jackal was observed not lifting a finger to help with the task, but claimed he was calculating the ratio of males to females in their kingdom.
The king discovered that the opposite was true: men outnumbered women in their land, and called out the jackal's deception.
The king dismissed the wild animals from his presence, realizing he had been outsmarted by the cunning jackal.
There were three brothers.
Two had no clothes and one no shirt.
The weather was very bad.
They decided to go shooting.
They took down three guns -- two were broken and one had no barrel -- and walked until they came to a meadow.
In the meadow, they saw a hare.
They began to fire at the hare but could not catch it.
They remembered that their godmother lived nearby and went to ask for help.
Their godmother was not at home, but she answered through a door, offering them three pots to cook the hare.
The brothers chose the pot with no bottom and put the hare in it to cook.
While cooking, they asked their godmother about her garden.
She offered them three walnut trees, two of which were dead and one that had never borne nuts.
One brother shook the tree with no nuts and a nut fell on his hat, breaking his heel.
They went to get the hare, which was cooked by then, and decided what to do with it.
Three brothers, Kofi, Kwame, and Kojo, huddled together, their bellies growling with hunger.
The rains had been relentless, and their crops were withering away like a leaf in autumn.
Kofi suggested going hunting to catch some food for their families.
However, Kwame pointed out that they had no clothes, and Kojo added that he had no shirt.
The three brothers set off into the wilderness, their footsteps quiet on the wet earth.
They stumbled upon a hare, its fur glistening with dew, but their shots fell short.
Kojo suggested going to their godmother Yaa's house for help.
Yaa instructed them to choose one of the three pots hanging on her kitchen wall, two of which were broken and one had no bottom.
The brothers chose the pot with no bottom and placed the hare inside to cook.
Kofi suggested asking Yaa if she had any walnuts in her garden, which led to Kwame shaking a barren tree and breaking his heel.
Three brothers from a small village in the mountains set out to go hunting for their family's dinner.
They stumbled upon a hare, but their guns were no match for it and it escaped into the underbrush.
The brothers remembered that their wise godmother lived nearby and went to her doorstep for help.
Their godmother offered to lend them a pot to cook the hare they had not caught.
Chonguita chose a broken pot with no bottom to cook the hare, and they set it over the fire.
As the sun began to set, one of the brothers suggested asking their godmother about her garden.
Chonguita went to shake a barren walnut tree, but a nut fell onto his hat and broke his heel.
The brothers picked up some nuts and returned to get the cooked hare, which they decided to take to the nearby village.
Chonguita put up a notice on the street that whoever wanted broth could come and receive it for free.
Many villagers came for the broth, but one stranger drank too much and nearly died from overindulgence.
The brothers sought out three physicians who lived in the village – one was blind, one deaf, and one dumb.
Chonguita visited the physicians, who each tried to diagnose his condition with absurd results.
The dumb physician scribbled a prescription that Chonguita read aloud, which instructed him to buy medicine from the apothecary.
Chonguita's cleverness saved the day once again, and he was known throughout the village as a master of wit and cunning.
A dragon living in the great sea saw that his wife's health was not good.
The dragon asked his wife what he could get for her to eat.
Mrs. Dragon said she wanted a monkey's heart to eat.
The dragon explained that monkeys lived in mountain forests and couldn't get one of their hearts.
Mrs. Dragon told the dragon that she knew she was going to die.
The dragon went on shore to find a monkey.
He spotted a monkey on top of a tree and called out to it.
The monkey replied that he wasn't afraid of falling from the tree.
The dragon offered to take the monkey across the sea to find more fruit and flowers.
The monkey got on the dragon's back, but the dragon suddenly dived down into the water.
The monkey asked where they were going, and the dragon explained that his wife was ill and wanted a monkey's heart.
The monkey realized he had left his heart on the tree and offered to get it for Mrs. Dragon.
The dragon returned to the shore with the monkey.
As they waited for the monkey to come down from the tree, Buddha revealed that he was actually the monkey at this time.
In days of yore, when the great sea stretched far and wide, there lived a mighty dragon who dwelled in its depths.
His wife, a gentle soul, lay bedridden, her vitality waning like the ebbing tide.
The loving husband beseeched her, 'My dear, what shall I fetch for you to eat?'
But she remained silent as the night, her eyes cast downward.
The dragon's heart swelled with devotion, and he pleaded once more, 'Just tell me, my love, and I shall bring it forthwith.'
She finally spoke, her voice barely above a whisper. 'I desire a monkey's heart to nourish mine own.'
The dragon set out on the shore, his massive form towering over the landscape.
He spied a mischievous monkey perched atop a tree, its tail swishing in the breeze.
The dragon called out, 'Hail, shining one! Are you not afraid of falling?'
The monkey replied with a grin, 'No, I have no such fear.'
A mighty dragon with a loving heart dwelt in the vast expanse of the ocean.
The dragon's wife lay ill and pale.
The dragon asked his wife what he could bring to ease her suffering.
His wife whispered that she desired a monkey's heart.
The dragon set off for the mountains to obtain the monkey's heart.
He spied a mischievous monkey perched atop a towering tree.
The dragon proposed that the monkey cross the sea and discover new forests.
The monkey agreed to climb on the dragon's back to cross the waves.
