As they set off across the waves, the dragon dived beneath the surface.
The monkey cried out in alarm and asked where he was being taken.
The dragon revealed that his wife needed the monkey's heart for her illness.
The monkey returned to the tree only to find that his heart was still perched upon its branch.
The monkey called out to the dragon below and asked him to wait while he fetched his heart.
As they parted ways, the great Buddha revealed his true form: he was the mischievous monkey.
The parents of a prince wanted him to marry.
He said, 'I will marry only such a woman about whom I can say with good conscience that she is the most sensitive woman in the world.'
His parents answered, 'Then go and find her!'
He went forth and came to a woman whose head was all bandaged up and who appeared to be suffering.
The prince asked, 'What is the matter with you?'
She said, 'This morning my maid was combing my hair, and she pulled one out, which is causing me great pain.'
He thought to himself, 'She isn't the right one. I will seek further.'
He went on his way and found another woman.
Her entire body was wrapped with the finest linen, and she looked very sad.
She said, 'Last night while I was in bed there was a little wrinkle in the sheet I was lying on, and it has made me sick.'
He thought, 'She is not the most sensitive one either. There must be a better one.'
He continued on his way and came to a third woman.
She was sitting in an easy chair and had a bandaged foot.
The prince said, 'You are the right one, for there cannot be a more sensitive woman than you!'
In the village of Azizi, where the air was sweet with the scent of blooming flowers, there lived a young prince named Kofi.
His parents, wise and just leaders, desired to see him married to a woman worthy of his love and devotion.
But Kofi was resolute: he would not settle for any bride until he had found one who could claim the title of 'the most sensitive heart in all the land'.
With determination etched on his face, Kofi set out on his journey, traversing villages and towns, meeting women from all walks of life.
His first stop was at the home of a woman named Akua, whose head was swathed in bandages.
Kofi asked her gently, 'What troubles you, dear one?'
Akua explained that her maid had been careless with her hair, yanking out a strand that now pained her terribly.
Kofi's heart went out to her, but he thought to himself, 'This is not the one I seek'.
Undeterred, he continued his search, arriving at the residence of a woman named Ama, whose body was wrapped in fine linens.
She appeared sorrowful, and when asked about her distress, revealed that a minor wrinkle in her bedsheet had caused her great discomfort.
Kofi's thoughts were the same as before: 'This is not the one I seek'.
His quest led him to the home of a woman named Efia, who sat in an easy chair, her foot bound with bandages.
Tears streamed down her face as she recounted how a gentle breeze had blown a jasmine petal onto her skin, causing her such anguish.
Kofi proposed to Efia, who accepted with joy, and they were married in a grand ceremony, surrounded by their friends and family.
A prince sought a bride who would be known throughout the land as the most sensitive woman in the world.
His parents urged him to find this paragon of sensitivity.
The prince set out on his quest, traveling through villages and towns.
He met a young woman whose head was swathed in bandages.
She explained that her maid had accidentally pulled out one of her hairpins while combing her hair.
The prince thought to himself, 'This is not the one I seek'.
He continued his journey and met a woman whose entire body was wrapped in fine silk.
She explained that a tiny wrinkle in her bedding had kept her awake all night.
The prince thought to himself, 'This is not the one I seek'.
He pressed on and met a woman sitting in an intricately carved chair with her foot bound with bandages.
She recounted how a gentle breeze had blown a jasmine petal onto her skin while strolling in the garden.
The prince was taken aback by her sensitivity and thought to himself, 'This is indeed the one I have been searching for'.
He proposed marriage on the spot, and the woman accepted.
The prince and his new bride returned to the kingdom, where they were welcomed with great fanfare.
Sennuccio and his wife Bedovina were seated near the door of their house to take the air.
Sennuccio asked Bedovina to shut the door, but she refused.
They made a bargain that whoever spoke first would shut the door.
Neither Sennuccio nor Bedovina dared to speak for fear of shutting the door.
Bedovina grew heavy with sleep and went to bed without speaking.
A serving-man passed through the street and entered Sennuccio's house looking for a light.
The serving-man found Sennuccio sitting on a bench with his eyes wide open but refused to speak.
The serving-man went into the inner room and found Bedovina lying alone in bed.
The serving-man shook Bedovina roughly, but she refused to speak or stir.
The serving-man lay down beside Bedovina and accomplished his task without her speaking.
When the young man had gone, Bedovina got out of bed and found Sennuccio still awake at the door.
Bedovina scolded Sennuccio for leaving her with the door open and not stopping the serving-man.
Sennuccio replied that Bedovina had lost their wager and should shut the door.
Bedovina shut the door and went to bed with Sennuccio.
Sennuccio and his wife Bedovina sat on their porch, sipping sweet tea under the shade of a baobab tree.
Sennuccio suggested making a bargain with Bedovina: whoever speaks first will shut the door.
Bedovina agreed to the bargain and they both sat back, waiting for the other to crack.
A young servant boy passed by, carrying a lantern with a flickering candle.
The flame went out, and the servant boy knocked on the door, calling out for light for his candle.
Sennuccio remained silent, too afraid to speak up lest he lose the wager.
The servant boy entered the house and found Sennuccio sitting on the porch.
The servant boy continued into the inner room and spotted Bedovina lying in bed, fast asleep.
Bedovina refused to stir, too afraid to speak up lest she lose the wager.
The servant boy stayed with Bedovina for a while and accomplished what needed to be done without her speaking.
When the servant boy left, Bedovina got out of bed and went to shut the door, only to find Sennuccio still sitting on the porch.
Bedovina chastised Sennuccio for leaving her vulnerable with the door wide open.
Sennuccio revealed that he had been tricked and was now equal with Bedovina in the wager.
Bedovina realized she had lost the game but also enjoyed a merry night with the young servant boy.
Sennuccio and his wife Bedovina sat idly by the entrance of their humble abode, reluctant to retire for the night.
Sennuccio bade his wife shut the door, but she demurred, insisting that he should take care of it himself.
Their petty squabble continued unabated, each one unwilling to yield, until finally, Sennuccio proposed a wager: whoever spoke first would be responsible for shutting the door.
Bedovina agreed to this proposition, but neither she nor her husband dared utter a word, lest they incur the penalty of having to shut the door.
A young serving-man, Chonguita by name, chanced upon Sennuccio's house while returning from his master's estate.
Chonguita sought refuge in the open doorway of Sennuccio's home and called out for anyone within.
Chonguita ventured further into the house, where he spotted Sennuccio sitting on a bench, his eyes wide awake but his mouth sealed shut.
The young man approached Chonguita cautiously and asked for a light to relight his lantern, but Sennuccio remained mute.
Chonguita continued on his way, noticing a faint glow emanating from the inner room and entering the bedchamber where he found Bedovina lying in bed.
Bedovina arose from bed and made her way to the entrance of their home after Chonguita departed.
She scolded Sennuccio for leaving her vulnerable to any unwanted visitor and failing to protect their home.
Sennuccio revealed that Bedovina was the one who must shut the door, as she had outwitted him in the wager.
Bedovina's face fell as she realized her loss, but a hint of mirth still lingered in her eyes.
With a resigned sigh, Bedovina rose to shut the door and then returned to bed beside her husband.
A poor farmer and his wife finished their day's labor and ate their supper.
A dispute arose between them as to who should shut the door, which had been blown open by a gust of wind.
The man said, 'Wife, shut the door!'
The woman replied, 'Husband, shut it yourself!'
The husband proposed that the one who spoke the first word should shut the door.
The wife agreed to this proposal and they retired in silence to bed.
In the middle of the night, a wild dog entered their room and began eating their food.
The couple remained silent as the dog ate and then left the house.
The next morning, the woman took some grain to a neighbor's house to have it ground into flour.
While she was away, the barber visited the farmer and asked why he was sitting alone.
The farmer remained silent as the barber shaved his head, half his beard, and half his mustache.
The barber then covered the farmer in lampblack, but still he did not speak.
The barber left, thinking the man was bewitched.
When the wife returned, she saw her husband's state and exclaimed, 'Ah! wretch, what have you been doing?'
An elderly couple lived in a small village nestled between two great rivers.
The couple tilled the land with their own hands.
A disagreement arose over who should close the door that had swung open in the wind.
The wife said her child should shut it.
The man refused to do so and proposed a contest of who spoke first.
The couple retired to their bed content in their silence.
A wild dog entered the room and devoured their meager store of food.
Neither of them spoke a word or tried to shoo the beast away.
The next morning, the barber came to visit the man.
The barber asked what was wrong but the farmer remained mute.
The barber shaved half of the farmer's beard and mustache.
The barber covered the farmer in a thick layer of lampblack.
The barber hastily departed, crying 'This man is cursed!'
The woman returned from the mill and saw her husband's transformed appearance.
An elderly couple sat by the fire after a long day's toil in their humble cottage.
The wind had blown open the door, and an argument ensued between them as to who should close it.
The husband said, 'Wife, see to the door!'
The wife retorted, 'Husband, you should tend to it.'
The man proposed, 'Let us not speak a word until one of us utters the first.'
His wife agreed, and they retired to their chamber in silence.
A fierce hound burst into the cottage, devouring the meager stores of food within.
Neither the husband nor wife dared utter a sound, lest they be forced to shut the door as per their earlier agreement.
The dog, sated, departed as suddenly as it arrived.
While the wife was away at the mill grinding grain into flour, a barber chanced upon the silent farmer.
The barber exclaimed, 'What sorcery is this?' observing the man's mute state.
He shaved the husband's head, then half his beard and mustache, but still the poor fellow remained speechless.
Finally, the barber daubed him in lampblack from head to toe.
The wife returned home, her eyes widening at the sight of her husband's grotesque appearance.
A husband and a wife were living together.
The husband asked his wife to make some fritters.
She replied that they didn't have a frying pan.
He told her to borrow one from his godmother.
The wife went to borrow the pan and returned with it.
She said that he had to carry the pan back when they were done with it.
They cooked the fritters and ate them.
The husband suggested that they both go to work and whoever spoke first would have to carry the pan back.
The wife started spinning while the husband drew his thread as a shoemaker.
They remained silent until the husband said 'Leulerò' while drawing his thread.
The wife responded with 'Piciciì, picicì, piciciò' while spinning.
A soldier passed by and asked a woman if there was a shoemaker in the street.
She took him to their house and he asked the husband to cut his horse's girth.
The husband remained silent until the soldier threatened to cut off his head if he didn't comply.
Anansi and his wife, Akua, sat beneath their thatched roof, planning to prepare some delicious fritters for their evening meal.
Akua asked Anansi what they would use to fry the fritters, as they had no pan of their own.
Anansi suggested that Akua borrow a pan from his mother's house, which was only a short walk away.
Akua returned with the borrowed pan and reminded Anansi to take it back when they were finished cooking.
After eating, Anansi proposed a challenge: whoever spoke first would have to carry the pan back to his mother's house.
Akua began spinning thread for her weaving while Anansi sat quietly drawing his own thread as he worked on a new pair of shoes.
A soldier with a majestic horse rode by and sought out Anansi's help in cutting a girth for his horse.
The soldier grew impatient when Anansi refused to speak first, only responding with 'Leulerò, leulerò'.
Akua chimed in with 'Piciciì, picicì, piciciò,' as she continued spinning her thread, but still did not break the silence.
The soldier's anger reached a boiling point and he seized his sword, ready to strike Anansi.
Akua cried out in distress, 'Ah! Don't take his life!' which broke the silence.
Anansi exclaimed, 'Now you go and carry the pan back to my mother, and I will attend to your horse!'
With a smile, Anansi took up the task and proved himself the victor once more.
As they returned home, Akua praised Anansi's wisdom, saying it was as vast as the rivers that flowed through their village.
Chonguita and her husband sat by their window, watching the sun rise over the rice fields.
Chonguita's husband asked to borrow a frying pan from his mother's house.
Chonguita borrowed the pan and returned home to find her husband preparing ingredients for their meal.
Chonguita's husband proposed a wager: whoever spoke first would carry back the pan.
A samurai on horseback rode by their house, asking if there was a skilled weaver nearby.
The samurai asked Chonguita's husband to weave a new coat for his horse, offering a generous reward.
Chonguita's husband replied that the threads were already tangled, and Chonguita chimed in that the silk was still raw.
The samurai grew impatient and threatened to cut off Chonguita's husband's head if he did not comply.
Chonguita cried out in distress, and her husband seized the opportunity to weave a magnificent coat for the samurai's horse.
The samurai was amazed by Chonguita's husband's skill and rewarded him handsomely.
A group of travelers passed by their house, admiring Chonguita's beautiful embroidery on a silk wall hanging.
One of the travelers asked Chonguita's husband to create an identical design for his own home, offering a handsome sum of money.
Chonguita's husband replied that the patterns were already set, and Chonguita chimed in that the threads were still raw.
In the end, it was clear that Chonguita's husband was the superior weaver, and he won the wager with his skill and patience.
There was a frightful drought.
The rivers after a while dried up and even the springs gave no water.
A great gathering of animals was held: Lion, Tiger, Wolf, Jackal, Elephant, all of them came together.
One had this plan, and another had that; but no plan seemed of value.
Finally one of them suggested: 'Come, let all of us go to the dry riverbed and dance; in that way we can tread out the water.'
The other animals danced and danced, and ultimately danced the water to the surface.
Rabbit did not dance with them.
So it was decided that Rabbit should have no water.
The following morning the animals saw the footprints of Rabbit in the ground, and Rabbit shouted to them: 'Aha! I did have some of the water, and it was most refreshing and tasted fine.'
Old Tortoise moved slowly forward, foot by foot: 'I will catch Rabbit.'
Tortoise's shell was covered with pitch, and he lay down at the edge of the water to resemble a stone.
Rabbit trod on Tortoise with his left foot, but it stuck fast.
Tortoise carried Rabbit back to the other animals, and they decided that Rabbit should die by taking him by his tail and dashing his head against a stone.
In a time of great drought, when rivers dried up and springs ran dry, the creatures of the land gathered to discuss the crisis.
A wise old Elephant proposed that they dance on the dry riverbed to bring forth water from the depths.
The other animals agreed, but one skeptical Rabbit refused to join in.
They danced with all their might, stomping and leaping until the earth trembled and water began to flow.
Rabbit scoffed at them, saying he would drink their water regardless of their efforts.
That evening, he snuck back to the riverbed and drank his fill.
The next morning, the animals discovered Rabbit's footprints in the mud, and they knew he had been there.
Old Tortoise spoke up with a plan to catch the cunning Rabbit.
Tortoise lay down at the river's edge, blending in with the stones, after being covered in pitch from the black beeswax tree.
Rabbit trod on Tortoise with his left foot, and it stuck fast in the pitch.
Tortoise slowly emerged from his shell, carrying Rabbit on his back.
The creatures cheered and jeered at Rabbit's predicament, calling out for him to admit defeat.
Lion was chosen to carry out the task of dashing Rabbit's head against a stone.
As Lion swung Rabbit around, his white skin began to slip off, revealing a coat of brilliant white fur underneath, and he was revealed as Chonguita, a master of disguise and deception.
The land was parched and dry, and not a drop of water could be found.
The creatures of the forest gathered 'neath the shade of an ancient tree to discuss the dire situation.
The tiger proposed a plan: Let us dance upon the riverbed, that we may tread out the water from beneath the earth!
Chonguita, the cunning rabbit, refused to join in the endeavor.
The other creatures danced on, and lo! The water began to rise from beneath their feet.
Chonguita watched with a sly smile as his companions drank their fill, but he remained aloof.
The next morning, the creatures discovered Chonguita's footprints upon the riverbed and knew that he had indeed drunk from their waters.
Old Tortoise proposed a plan to catch Chonguita by rubbing his shell with pitch.
Tortoise lay down at the edge of the water, drawing his head into his shell, and waited for Chonguita to arrive.
Chonguita was caught by Tortoise's plan and became stuck to the pitch.
Tortoise carried Chonguita on his back back to the other creatures.
The creatures debated what to do with the cunning rabbit, but he pleaded for a shameful death.
Chonguita was freed when Lion's mane slipped off from his tail during the attempt to carry out the task.
Brer Rabbit could never get any peace due to Brer Wolf making raids on his family.
Brer Rabbit built a straw house, but it was torn down by Brer Wolf.
Brer Rabbit then made a pine top house, which also got raided by Brer Wolf.
Next, Brer Rabbit built a bark house, but it too was attacked and destroyed by Brer Wolf.
Each time his house was destroyed, one of Brer Rabbit's children was taken away by Brer Wolf.
Brer Rabbit finally got angry and built a plank house with a rock foundation to protect himself and his family.
The plank house gave Brer Rabbit some peace and quiet, allowing him to socialize with neighbors and enjoy domestic life.
Brer Wolf laid low after seeing the new house, but the little rabbits remained skittish.
One day, Brer Rabbit heard a commotion outside and saw Brer Wolf running into his house, pursued by dogs.
Brer Rabbit hid Brer Wolf in the chest and pretended to help him, but was actually planning revenge.
Brer Rabbit tricked Brer Wolf by pouring hot water onto the chest lid, causing him to be scalded.
The scalding water killed Brer Wolf, and Brer Rabbit celebrated with a jubilee attended by his neighbors.
Brer Rabbit's house became famous for displaying Brer Wolf's hide on the back porch as a trophy.
A clever rabbit named Chonguita lived in a small village with his family of little rabbits.
Their joy was often disrupted by the thieving wolf, Hsiang Li.
Chonguita's home was always being raided by Hsiang Li, who would steal some of his children each time he tore down one of Chonguita's houses.
The rabbit grew tired of this and decided to build a new home that would be safe from the wolf's pranks.
He constructed a sturdy house made of planks with a rock foundation, which finally gave him peace.
One day when Chonguita was preparing to visit the beautiful raccoon lady, Mei Mei, he heard a commotion outside.
Hsiang Li had burst into their home, covered in mud and panting heavily.
Chonguita chuckled and hid Hsiang Li in a chest, pretending to be concerned about the dogs chasing him outside.
As Hsiang Li began to stir inside the chest, Chonguita pretended to make him a cup of tea and bored holes in the chest lid for air.
Chonguita poured hot water onto the chest lid, scalding Hsiang Li and ending his threat to Chonguita's family.
The villagers gathered at Chonguita's house to celebrate their victory over Hsiang Li.
From that day on, the wolf's hide hung proudly in the back porch as a reminder of his defeat.
In days of yore, a wily rabbit named Chonguita lived with his family in a cozy little burrow.
Br'er Wolf, a cunning canine, was always on the prowl to pilfer one of Chonguita's precious young'uns.
Chonguita built himself a straw hut thinking it would be safe from Br'er Wolf's prying eyes.
Br'er Wolf tore down the straw hut with ease and Chonguita lost one of his little ones in the process.
Chonguita constructed another dwelling out of pine tops but Br'er Wolf razed it to the ground as well.
Br'er Wolf claimed another young rabbit for his own and Chonguita's patience wore thin.
Chonguita enlisted the aid of skilled carpenters to build a new home with a rock foundation that would withstand Br'er Wolf's attempts.
The little rabbits could finally live in peace and quiet while Chonguita strolled about without fear of Br'er Wolf's raids.
Br'er Wolf burst into the house begging for Chonguita's mercy from those ravenous dogs that pursued him.
Chonguita directed Br'er Wolf to jump into a nearby chest and secured the wolf inside with a hook.
Chonguita bored holes in the chest lid with a gimlet to give Br'er Wolf some air.
Br'er Wolf let out a final pitiful cry before succumbing to his scalding fate from the water seeping through the holes.
Chonguita summoned his neighbors to celebrate Br'er Wolf's demise and hung his pelt proudly in the back porch of Chonguita's house.
Hlakanyana met a boy tending some goats.
The boy had a digging-stick with him.
Hlakanyana proposed that they should pursue after birds, and the boy agreed.
They pursued birds the whole day.
In the evening, when the sun set, Hlakanyana said, 'It is time now to roast our birds.'
Hlakanyana said, 'We must go under the water and see who will come out last.'
They went under the water, and Hlakanyana came out last.
The cunning fellow said, 'Let us try again.' They boy agreed to that.
Hlakanyana came out quickly and ate all the birds. He left the heads only.
Then he went under the water again. The boy came out while he was still under the water.
A young boy tending to his flock of goats was approached by a cunning trickster named Hlakanyana.
Hlakanyana proposed that they join forces in pursuit of birds, and the boy agreed to his plan.
As the sun began its descent into the western sky, Hlakanyana suggested that they take a break by the riverbank to roast their birds.
Hlakanyana proposed that they test their bravery by diving into the river and seeing who would emerge last.
The boy agreed to the challenge, but Hlakanyana emerged from the water first and accused the boy of cheating.
Hlakanyana claimed that the boy had eaten all the birds while still under water and demanded his digging-stick as compensation.
The boy reluctantly handed over the digging-stick, which Hlakanyana then lent to a group of potters in exchange for their help with their clay.
However, the digging-stick broke in two when used by the potters, and Hlakanyana laughed at their misfortune.
Hlakanyana accepted a pot from the potters as compensation for the broken tool and warned them of its fate.
He then lent the pot to some boys tending to goats, but it shattered into pieces when used by them.
Hlakanyana laughed again and accepted a goat from the boys as compensation for the broken pot.
He then lent the goat to some keepers of calves, but it died soon after being taken possession of by them.
Hlakanyana laughed once more and accepted a calf from the keepers as compensation for the dead goat.
Finally, he lent the calf to some keepers of cows, but it died soon after being taken possession of by them.
A cunning fellow named Hlakanyana roamed the land, seeking adventure and mischief.
Hlakanyana chanced upon a young lad tending to his flock of goats.
Hlakanyana proposed a game: 'Let us chase after birds together!'
They pursued the feathered creatures for hours, their laughter echoing through the valleys and hills.
Hlakanyana declared, 'Now is the time to roast our quarry!' and suggested a diving challenge.
Hlakanyana emerged first from the river, feigning surprise that he had won.
The boy protested when Hlakanyana ate all the birds, leaving only their heads behind.
Hlakanyana insisted that the boy pay for his birds with his digging-stick.
Hlakanyana arrived at a village where potters were busy shaping clay into vessels and proposed to lend him their hands.
The first time the artisans used the digging-stick, it broke in two.
Hlakanyana claimed that the potters had broken his digging-stick, which he received from his companion.
The potters offered Hlakanyana a pot as compensation for the broken digging-stick.
Hlakanyana carried the pot to a nearby village and proposed that the young boys lend him their vessel.
The pot shattered into pieces while in Hlakanyana's care, and he claimed it was broken by the boys.
A hermit found a large cave hidden from view while walking through the desert.
The hermit saw gold shining brightly inside the cave and immediately ran out of the cave in fear.
The hermit met three ruffians who were hiding nearby to rob travelers.
The ruffians stopped the hermit and asked him why he was fleeing, but he replied that Death was chasing him.
The hermit led the ruffians back to the cave and showed them the gold, which they believed was Death.
The three ruffians congratulated themselves on finding the treasure and dismissed the hermit.
The ruffians decided to stay at the cave and guard the wealth until they could carry it away.
One of the ruffians suggested that one of them should go to the city to sell some of the gold for supplies.
The devil put a plan in the heart of the ruffian who went to the city to poison his companions and keep the treasure for himself.
The poisoned food was brought back to the cave and eaten by the two remaining ruffians, who then died.
The ruffian who had gone to the city also died after eating the poisoned food.
The hermit returned to his affairs, unaware of what had happened to the three ruffians.
The gold remained untouched and unguarded, still shining brightly in the cave.
A hermit wandered through the scorching desert, seeking refuge from the heat.
He entered a vast cave hidden by dunes and found a treasure trove of gold shining in the darkness.
The hermit fled the cave, fearing that such wealth came with a price.
He encountered three rough men who haunted the desert, preying on travelers like vultures.
The men lay in wait, but seeing no one pursuing the hermit, they grew wary and asked him what chased him.
The hermit led them back to the cave, pointing out the glittering gold as if it were a specter.
The three men gazed upon the treasure in awe and congratulated themselves on their good fortune.
However, they soon turned to how they might enjoy the wealth for themselves, with one suggesting sending someone to sell some of it in the city.
The treacherous man among them secretly planned to poison his companions once he returned from the city.
As soon as the poisoned food was consumed, the companions struck down their unsuspecting friend and enjoyed the wealth for themselves.
However, they all fell dead, including the treacherous one, consumed by their own greed and deceit.
The hermit had fled the cave, leaving the treasure untouched, as a testament to the power of wisdom.
The people of the nearby village whispered among themselves about the fate of the three men and warned others against seeking to harm others.
A humble hermit chanced upon an enormous cave hidden from view in a desolate wasteland.
The hermit entered the cave to rest and was immediately struck by the radiant glow emanating from within.
The cave's interior was filled with an abundance of gold, shining brightly like the sun.
The hermit fled the cave as if pursued by a malevolent spirit without hesitation.
Three nefarious bandits who had been lurking in the area noticed the hermit's hasty departure and decided to investigate further.
The hermit revealed that he was fleeing from Death itself when confronted by the bandits.
The bandits demanded to see this 'Death' for themselves, but the hermit feared their intentions and reluctantly led them back to the cave.
Upon entering the cave once more, the bandits were awestruck by the treasure trove of gold before them.
The three bandits remained behind, bickering over what to do with their newfound wealth.
A cunning third bandit secretly planned to poison his companions' food, allowing him to claim the treasure for himself once they were dead.
The three bandits agreed to send one of their number to a nearby city to purchase provisions and supplies.
Upon returning from the city, he brought with him poisoned food, which he shared among his companions without their knowledge.
The bandits succumbed to the deadly effects of the tainted victuals, one by one, in a fit of jealousy and greed.
A husband and a wife lived together; the husband was a tailor, the wife also a tailor and a good housekeeper.
The husband found some things in the kitchen broken: pots, glasses, plates.
He asked his wife, 'How were they broken?'
She answered, 'How do I know?' and he pressed her for an answer.
The wife said she broke them with the scissors, in anger.
The husband was skeptical and wanted to verify this claim.
He tied a rope around his wife and began to lower her into the well.
As she was lowered, he asked her again how she broke the items.
She continued to insist that it was with the scissors, even as she was being lowered further into the well.
The husband let go of the rope, and his wife fell all the way into the water.
He realized too late that he had made a mistake and tried to pull her out.
When he pulled her out, she still refused to admit how she broke the items.
A tailor and his wife lived in a small hut in the village of Azul.
Kofi was known for his skillful hands that stitched together beautiful kente cloth.
His wife Ama was no less talented, with fingers that moved swiftly as she sewed and mended their family's clothes.
One day, Kofi returned from his market trip to find their kitchen in disarray.
Pots lay shattered on the floor, glasses were broken, and plates were cracked.
Kofi asked Ama, 'What happened here? Who broke these things?'
Ama replied, 'How do I know?' Kofi's patience wore thin as he pressed her for an answer.
Kofi decided to test her story by tying a rope around her waist and lowering her into their well.
As she descended, Ama refused to back down, repeating like a mantra: 'I did it with the scissors.'
Kofi's rage boiled over, and he let out a mighty shout, releasing the rope, and Ama plunged into the well.
The villagers gathered around, whispering among themselves: 'Ama is stubborn as a mule.'
Kofi said, 'I'll let her be for now. Maybe when she's hungry and thirsty, she'll tell me the truth.'
As the sun dipped below the horizon, casting the village in a warm orange glow, Ama looked up at Kofi with eyes that seemed to say: 'You may have won this battle, but I'll never give in.'
A humble tailor and his wife lived in a small village nestled between two great mountains.
Their tranquil life was disrupted by the discovery of shattered dishes and broken pots.
The husband's anger flared like a wildfire as he confronted his wife about the broken items.
The wife insisted that she had used her scissors to break the things, despite her husband's skepticism.
In a fit of rage, the husband tied a rope around his wife's waist and began to lower her into the well at the edge of their village.
As she descended deeper into the well, the wife's obstinacy only intensified, and she continued to deny using anything other than her scissors.
The husband finally lowered his wife into the water, where she disappeared beneath the surface of the well.
The husband stood at the edge of the well, his heart heavy with sorrow and regret for his rash actions.
He realized too late that he had been deceived by his own anger and called out to his wife, asking if she was satisfied now.
The wife's hand emerged from the water, her fingers moving in a swift, cutting motion as if she were still wielding scissors.
The husband's eyes widened in understanding and horror as he realized his wife had been telling the truth all along.
He pulled his wife out of the well, but it was too late to undo the damage to their relationship.
The husband and wife stood together on dry land, with the wife's silence speaking louder than any words could have.
